name: Kuala Lumpur International Airport (WMKK/KUL)
oneLiner: Departure airport; MH370 took off from runway 32R (threshold 2.74739N 101.722E) at 00:42 (UTC+8), 8 Mar 2014.
source: OurAirports airports.csv + runways.csv (raw.githubusercontent.com/davidmegginson/ourairports-data); takeoff time FI p1 via Gilbert PDF (vridar.org)

name: Penang International Airport (WMKP/PEN)
oneLiner: Military radar tracked MH370 passing 6 NM south of Penang Island at 01:52:35 (UTC+8) during the turnback.
source: OurAirports airports.csv; 17:52:35 event FI p7 via Gilbert PDF (vridar.org)

name: Kota Bharu (Sultan Ismail Petra Airport, WMKC)
oneLiner: MH370 crossed the peninsula coast just north of Kota Bharu at ~01:37 (UTC+8) on the turnback leg; the airport's own primary radar recorded the pass at ~4 s resolution (last inbound return 01:36:54 (UTC+8)).
source: OurAirports airports.csv; coast-crossing time FI p7 via Gilbert PDF (vridar.org), mh370search.com gives 17:36:43; PSR returns from ATC-Caption-2025 decode (fetched 2026-07-16)

name: Waypoint IGARI
oneLiner: Last waypoint passed under secondary radar (01:20:31 (UTC+8)); transponder ceased and the turnback began just beyond it.
source: Wikipedia MH370 article (6°56'12"N 103°35'06"E), cross-checked with FI flight plan table N0656.2 E10335.1 via mh370wiki.net

name: Waypoint BITOD
oneLiner: Next filed waypoint after IGARI; MH370 turned back before reaching it.
source: FI filed flight plan table (N0715.4 E10407.1) via mh370wiki.net Waypoints_and_MH370

name: Waypoint GOLUD
oneLiner: One of a cluster of waypoints (with ABTOK, KADAX) near Kota Bharu that the turnback track passed over or near.
source: OpenNav (6°17'06"N 102°16'39"E); passed-near statement mh370wiki.net History of the flight

name: Waypoint PUKAR
oneLiner: Waypoint near Butterworth passed on the peninsula crossing toward Penang.
source: OpenNav (5°28'43"N 100°43'14"E); passed-near statement mh370wiki.net History of the flight

name: Waypoint VAMPI
oneLiner: Airway N571 waypoint in the Strait of Malacca; primary radar tracked MH370 passing it at 02:13 (UTC+8).
source: OpenNav (6°10'56"N 97°35'08"E); 18:13:00 event FI p7 via Gilbert PDF (vridar.org)

name: Waypoint MEKAR
oneLiner: N571 waypoint; the last primary radar fix at 02:22:12 (UTC+8) lies 10 NM beyond it toward NILAM.
source: OpenNav (6°30'14"N 96°29'28"E); cross-validated geometrically against the published 10-NM-past-MEKAR fix 6.577655N 96.340864E (mh370search/radiantphysics)

name: Waypoint NILAM
oneLiner: Next N571 waypoint beyond MEKAR; radar contact was lost before MH370 reached it.

name: Waypoint IDKUT
oneLiner: Waypoint northwest of NILAM, relevant to post-02:22 (UTC+8) path hypotheses (beyond recorded epochs).

name: Last secondary radar contact (01:21:13 UTC+8)
oneLiner: SSR position symbol dropped off the KL ATCC display 3.2 NM past IGARI (SIR Table 2.2C); end of Epoch 1. The last actual SSR return was ~30 s earlier: 01:20:42 (UTC+8), 6.9518N 103.5978E (Kota Bharu radar head).
source: time + '3.2 nm after passing IGARI': SIR Table 2.2C p.344 / FI p2; position interpolated ~42 s past IGARI on 059° toward BITOD (official reports publish no coordinate); last actual return from ATC-Caption-2025 KB_SSR decode (fetched 2026-07-16)

name: Kate Tee yacht sighting (~03:10-03:25 UTC+8)
short: Uncorroborated eyewitness: sailor reported a low aircraft with 'black smoke' from her yacht, ~03:10 (UTC+8)-03:25 (UTC+8).
oneLiner: Sailor Kate Tee reported a low-flying aircraft 'that appeared to be descending' with 'black smoke coming from the tail' from her yacht at ~6°37.7'N 94°26.4'E; her report gives initial sighting ~03:10 (UTC+8), closest approach ~03:20 (UTC+8)-03:25 (UTC+8) (the UGIB 2020 model instead puts MH370's closest approach at 02:53:46 (UTC+8)). Uncorroborated eyewitness account.
source: Position and quotes: saucysailoress.wordpress.com/2017/02/02/2299/ (Gibson-assisted report, fetched 2026-07-16, HTTP 200); timing caveat per the same report vs UGIB 2020 App. E.3.5

name: Last primary radar fix (02:22:12 UTC+8)
oneLiner: Final military radar detection, 10 NM past MEKAR on N571 at ~29,500 ft; end of Epoch 2 (alternative Lido-slide position: 6.871123N 97.354047E).
source: mh370search.com / radiantphysics (Iannello), SIR-figure interpretation; altitude Wikipedia MH370 article

name: Pulau Perak
oneLiner: Small island in the Strait of Malacca; military radar recorded MH370 passing it at 02:02 (UTC+8).
source: Wikipedia Pulau Perak (5°40'50"N 98°56'27"E); 18:02 time Wikipedia MH370 article

name: HA01 Cape Leeuwin hydroacoustic station (hydrophone H01W1)
oneLiner: CTBTO IMS hydrophone triad ~100 km SW of Cape Leeuwin whose recordings were analysed by Curtin University for MH370 impact signals.
source: H01W1 coordinates from Table 1 of PMC10028758 (Royal Society Open Science COVID ocean-noise study); role per mh370search.com and CTBTO

name: RAAF Learmonth Airport (YPLM)
oneLiner: Western Australian air base used as a staging point for RAAF/RNZAF search sorties over the southern Indian Ocean.
source: OurAirports airports.csv

name: Inmarsat ground earth station, Perth (PITC)
oneLiner: Ground station at the Perth International Telecommunications Centre that logged the hourly satellite handshakes defining the BTO/BFO arcs.
source: site coordinates (-31°48'09"S 115°53'07"E) from ESA Perth station page (co-located at PITC); Inmarsat GES role per Wikipedia MH370 satellite communications

name: Inmarsat-3 F1 nominal sub-satellite point
oneLiner: IOR satellite relaying MH370's handshakes; nominally geostationary at 64.5°E, drifting in an oval between 1.6°S-1.6°N and 64.45-64.58°E.
source: Wikipedia: Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 satellite communications

name: 7th arc - CSIRO/ATSB most-likely crash region
oneLiner: CSIRO drift-analysis point (35.6°S 92.8°E) on the 7th BTO arc, identified with 'unprecedented precision'; centre of the priority search region.
source: Wikipedia MH370 article, cross-checked with CSIRO news release (csiro.au, Aug 2017)

name: Broken Ridge
oneLiner: Rugged oceanic plateau in the SE Indian Ocean crossed by the 7th arc; complicated the underwater search bathymetry.
source: Wikipedia Broken Ridge (listed centre 31°S 95°E; feature ~1,200 km long)

name: Flaperon find site, Saint-André, Réunion
oneLiner: First confirmed MH370 debris - a right flaperon found on a Saint-André beach on 29 July 2015, ~4,000 km west of the search area.
source: commune coordinates Wikipedia Saint-André, Réunion (20°57'38"S 55°39'02"E; beach on NE shore, exact spot not published); find details Wikipedia MH370 / Search articles

name: Near-miss: Emirates EK407 / Etihad EY461 (~04:02 UTC+8)
short: MODEL-DEPENDENT (CAPTIO path only): ~12 NM below the Emirates EK407 / Etihad EY461 formation, ~04:02 (UTC+8).
plain: Around 04:02 (UTC+8) two airliners flying close together - an Emirates A380 bound for Dubai just above an Etihad jet bound for Abu Dhabi, both out of Melbourne - crossed this area. If MH370 was on the route proposed by the CAPTIO researchers, it passed roughly 12 nautical miles below them in the dark. The crews were close enough to have seen its lights, yet none reported anything, which the researchers take as a sign the aircraft was flying unlit. This matches only the CAPTIO route; on the other leading theories the plane was 100 to 260 nautical miles away at that moment.
oneLiner: MODEL-DEPENDENT. On the CAPTIO (Blelly/Marchand) reconstructed path, MH370 passed ~12 NM below two Melbourne-departing widebodies flying in vertical formation - Emirates EK407 (A380, to Dubai, FL360->380) directly above Etihad EY461 (to Abu Dhabi, FL320->340), near airway N509 at ~04:02 (UTC+8). Neither crew reported seeing MH370, consistent with lights off on a moonless night (CAPTIO's argument). The encounter EXISTS ONLY IF MH370 flew the CAPTIO route: under UGIB 2020 the aircraft would be ~266 NM away, under Ashton 2015 ~102 NM. It is a consistency check for CAPTIO's FL300 track, not independent proof.
source: CAPTIO, 'Was visual contact between MH370 and other flights possible after the FMT?' (Marchand & Blelly, v1.a, 6 Apr 2025), Table 1: ETD461 min 12 NM at 20:02:10, UAE407 min 12 NM at 20:02:15, both at 4.34 S 93.24 E, 'less than 2 Nm interval ... UAE407 directly above ETD461'. Distances measured against the Blelly/Marchand reference trajectory. FlightAware-derived traffic data. https://www.mh370-caption.net/wp-content/uploads/Caption-analysis-MH370-flights-after-FMT.pdf . Also nearby per CAPTIO Table 1: Emirates EK359 (~39 NM, 19:19) and Singapore SQ478 (>85 NM); South African SA280 eliminated (too far). Capt Martyn Smith's separately-reported EK407 sighting was on the 8 Mar (not 7 Mar UTC) flight and is not this encounter.

name: Mike McKay oil-rig eyewitness report (~01:20-01:30 UTC+8)
short: Uncorroborated eyewitness: oil-rig worker reported a burning object to the west; not substantiated (early false lead).
oneLiner: Uncorroborated. New Zealand oil-rig worker Mike McKay, aboard the Songa Mercur rig off Vung Tau, Vietnam, emailed his employer that on the night of the disappearance he saw a burning object at high altitude to the west (bearing ~265-275 deg) over ~10-15 seconds. Investigated and not substantiated; the position is in the South China Sea near the initial (wrong) search area, well east of the SATCOM-derived track. Kept as a documented early false lead.
source: Rig surface position 08°22'30.2"N 108°42'22.3"E and account widely reported March 2014 (McKay's emailed report to his employer, forwarded to Vietnamese/Malaysian authorities); coordinate provenance flagged, eyewitness account unverified. Cross-check: Wikipedia MH370 / contemporary news coverage, retrieved 2026-07-17

name: Co-pilot's phone registers on Penang cell tower (~01:52 UTC+8)
short: Debated: co-pilot Fariq's mobile briefly detected by a Penang tower during the flyby (~01:52 (UTC+8)). No call/text.
oneLiner: During the Strait of Malacca flyby, first officer Fariq Abdul Hamid's mobile phone was reportedly detected (a brief registration, not a call or text) by a telecommunications tower on Penang Island around 01:52 (UTC+8) - the only such contact from any of the 239 aboard. It indicates the phone was on and the aircraft flew low enough for line-of-sight to a tower, but yielded no location beyond 'near Penang'. Reported by Malaysian officials/media in 2014 and debated; not emphasised in the final SIR. Coordinate is Penang Island (specific tower not published).
source: Reported April-May 2014 (e.g. New Straits Times / Reuters coverage of the co-pilot's phone 'connecting' to a Penang tower ~01:52 MYT during the turnback); the SIR (2018) discusses cellular-registration analysis without confirming a firm detection. Coordinate = Penang Island centroid (tower not published), flagged. Retrieved 2026-07-17

name: Final voice contact (01:19:30 UTC+8)
short: Last words from the cockpit, at the KL-to-Ho-Chi-Minh ATC handoff, ~90 s before the transponder stopped.
oneLiner: At 01:19:30 (UTC+8) Kuala Lumpur Area Control told MH370 to contact Ho Chi Minh City radio on 120.9 MHz; the reply 'Good night, Malaysian Three Seven Zero' was the aircraft's last transmission. About 90 seconds later, near waypoint IGARI (01:21 UTC+8), the transponder ceased and the turnback began. The released ATC recording is third-party/copyrighted (no public-domain audio file exists), so it is linked rather than mirrored; the authoritative transcript is in the ICAO-hosted preliminary report.
source: ATC transcript: Malaysia MOT / ICAO Preliminary Report (2014), verified PDF HTTP 200; handoff frequency and wording per the released DCA ATC recording (May 2014). Retrieved 2026-07-17

name: Waypoint BEDAX
short: Waypoint ~100 NM west of Banda Aceh. In the leading southern-route model (UGIB 2020) the aircraft turns due south here, its longitude (E93.79) coinciding with the 7th-arc crash estimate.
oneLiner: Air-navigation waypoint about 100 NM west of Banda Aceh, Sumatra, near longitude E93.79. In the leading southern-route reconstructions (UGIB 2020) the final major turn ends at BEDAX and the aircraft then tracks due south (180 deg true, toward the South Pole) along this meridian to fuel exhaustion, so the waypoint's longitude coincides with the estimated 7th-arc impact longitude. It sits beyond the recorded radar epochs: a route-model anchor, not a recorded fix.
source: OpenNav / mh370wiki.net Waypoints (BEDAX 5.364330N 93.78758E, ~100 NM west of Banda Aceh); role as final-major-turn / due-south anchor per UGIB 2020, 'The Final Resting Place of MH370' (mh370.radiantphysics.com/papers). Retrieved 2026-07-17

name: Captain's home-simulator southern-Indian-Ocean endpoint (recovered sim data)
short: Deleted data recovered from Captain Zaharie's home flight simulator held a saved session up the Malacca Strait then deep into the southern Indian Ocean, ending near here with fuel exhausted. Significance contested.
oneLiner: FBI/RMP forensics recovered deleted Microsoft Flight Simulator files (reconstructed from a 3 Feb 2014 volume shadow copy, about five weeks before the flight) from Captain Zaharie Ahmad Shah's home simulator. The saved points ran northwest up the Malacca Strait, turned south, and ended in the deep southern Indian Ocean; the final points (labelled 45S1/45S2) lie near 45 deg S 104 deg E with the simulated altitude manually set low and the fuel exhausted. It is the only documented instance of the captain simulating a southern-ocean flight to fuel exhaustion, central to the human-intervention hypothesis, but the saved points are a discontinuous manual simulation, not a flight plan or a recorded track, and their significance is contested.
source: RMP forensic report as detailed by the ATSB and reported by Simon Hradecky (Aviation Herald) / FlightGlobal, 2016; recovered simulator points 45S1/45S2 near 45S 104E. Cross-check: 'The Atlantic' (William Langewiesche, 2019) and New York Magazine coverage of the RMP simulator data. Coordinates are simulator artifacts, not a claimed impact point. Retrieved 2026-07-17
